title: From Deposition Stress to Surface Reactivity: Strain-Dependent Hydrogen Evolution on Sputtered Platinum Thin Films
authors: Sabrina Baha, Alejandro E. Perez Mendoza, Leonardo H. Morais, Aleksander Kostka, Shivam Shukla, Ellen Suhr, Andre Oliveira, Annika Gatzki, Henrik H. Kristoffersen, Jan Rossmeisl, Corina Andronescu, Alfred Ludwig
abstract: Strain has emerged as a promising approach for tuning electrocatalytic properties, yet its role in sputter-deposited thin films remains poorly understood. In this work, magnetron-sputtered platinum (Pt) thin films with different stress states were prepared by varying the sputter pressure. The resulting changes in microstructure, residual strain, and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity were investigated using complementary characterization techniques and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Structural analysis reveals a transition of (111)-textured Pt thin films from dense and smooth films at low pressures, to more porous microstructures with increased roughness at higher pressures. Electrochemical measurements show that films deposited at low sputter pressure exhibit the highest HER activity, while higher sputter pressures lead to reduced activity despite increased surface area. DFT calculations demonstrate that lattice strain alters hydrogen adsorption energetics and surface coverage on Pt(111), providing a mechanistic explanation for the observed activity trends. Overall, the results highlight that HER activity in sputtered Pt thin films is governed by the interplay of residual strain, microstructure, and hydrogen coverage.
summary: This paper demonstrates how controlling the sputtering pressure of platinum thin films allows for the tuning of residual strain, which directly impacts electrocatalytic performance. It reveals that compressive strain optimizes hydrogen adsorption for the hydrogen evolution reaction, providing a way to engineer catalysts via deposition parameters.
main_problem: The study investigates how deposition-induced residual strain and microstructural changes in magnetron-sputtered platinum thin films influence the Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) activity.
main_result: Low sputter pressure films exhibit the highest HER activity due to compressive strain that optimizes hydrogen adsorption energy, whereas higher pressures lead to reduced activity as tensile strain and increased surface roughness/porosity dominate.
method: The researchers used RF magnetron sputtering to tune stress, Digital Holographic Microscopy and XRD for strain/stress analysis, electrochemical measurements (LSV, CV, EIS) for activity, and DFT calculations to model hydrogen adsorption energetics.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of magnetron-sputtered platinum (Pt) thin films (60-70 nm) with a Tantalum adhesion layer on Silicon substrates, where sputter pressure is used to control the transition between compressive and tensile stress states.
key_observables: Residual in-plane and out-of-plane strain, surface roughness (Ra,Rq), electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), hydrogen adsorption free energy (delta G_H), and HER overpotential.
important_parameters: Sputter pressure (0.13 to 3.33 Pa), biaxial in-plane strain, and hydrogen surface coverage.
main_assumptions: Thermal stress contribution from substrate heating is considered negligible; biaxial modulus was approximated using single-crystal elastic constants for Pt(111).
figures_summary: XRD patterns and out-of-plane strain vs. pressure; TEM images of morphology transition; correlation of lattice parameters with pressure; CV curves and ECSA/roughness trends; HER polarization curves and theoretical hydrogen adsorption free energy landscapes.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing the link between sputter pressure and residual stress, moves to characterizing the resulting microstructural and crystallographic changes, evaluates the electrochemical HER performance, and concludes with DFT calculations to provide a mechanistic thermodynamic explanation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
